perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party acknowledges that it is aware and that the Receiving Party and its Representatives have been advised that the United States securities laws prohibit any person having non-public material information about a company from purchasing or selling securities of that company or from communicating such information to any other person under circumstances in which it is reasonably foreseeable that such person is likely to purchase or sell such securities.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement represents the entire understanding and agreement of the parties hereto with respect to the matters contained herein, and may be amended, modified or waived only by a separate writing executed by the Receiving Party and the Company expressly so amending, modifying or waiving this Agreement. The Receiving Party acknowledges that it is aware and that the Receiving Party and its Representatives have been advised that the United States securities laws prohibit any person having non-public material information about a company from purchasing or selling securities of that company or from communicating such information to any other person under circumstances in which it is reasonably foreseeable that such person is likely to purchase or sell such securities.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the securities law acknowledgement to the 'Entire Agreement' section buries it within general contract terms, making it less conspicuous and harder to locate quickly. It is no longer clearly highlighted as a standalone warning, which could impact comprehension and compliance.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934, Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 10b-5 prohibits employing any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, making any untrue statement of a material fact, or omitting a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading in connection with the purchase or sale of any security. By burying the acknowledgement of securities laws within the entire agreement clause, it obfuscates the importance of the insider trading prohibition, increasing the risk that parties will violate the rule due to a lack of clear, prominent warning.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... perturbation.justification: The modified text moves the acknowledgement of securities laws into the 'Entire Agreement' section. This placement, as explained in the Perturbation Explanation, reduces the prominence and visibility of the securities law warning. The Law Explanation argues this obfuscates the importance of insider trading prohibitions. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically 17 CFR § 240.10b-5, prohibits any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, making any untrue statement of a material fact, or omitting a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading. While the acknowledgement isn't entirely omitted, burying it can be seen as employing a 'device' or 'scheme' that effectively omits the importance of the information, increasing the risk of violating the rule. Placing critical information within a standard boilerplate section diminishes its impact and clarity, potentially misleading parties about its significance. This aligns with the definition of 'Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction,' where a required legal disclosure is placed in a misleading section.
